summary: Graph DB as OFFICE storage engine — Memory Den + Atlas + Finder collapse into one graph. Exploration, not locked.

content.decision: Graph DB is the candidate storage engine for OFFICE Memory Den, replacing the loosely-assumed MongoDB. Memories are a graph structurally: tags are edges, provenance is directed, scope is hierarchical, recall is traversal.
content.context: Discussion about JSON query cost at high volume. Document stores (MongoDB) don't solve the bottleneck — they just move JSON to a server. SQL adds relational complexity that doesn't fit memories. Graph is the third paradigm: associative recall, native to how memories connect.
content.why: Memory Den + Atlas + Finder collapse into one graph engine. Arbiter becomes a pattern match query. Recaller becomes traversal + ABAC filter. index.json becomes unnecessary. Fewer moving parts = easier customer self-hosting = better on-prem story.
alternatives_considered: MongoDB — natural JSON fit but doesn't solve query cost at scale, adds ops burden without collapsing components
PostgreSQL + JSONB — strong query engine but relational model doesn't fit memory topology, graph traversal via recursive CTEs is awkward
Vector DB — good for semantic recall but doesn't model relationships, would need companion for graph/FTS
Git repo as central store — works for small teams, good open-source fork path, but not enterprise scale for concurrent cross-team access
impact: OFFICE architecture simplifies: Memory Den + Atlas + Finder → one graph engine
Core stays untouched — JSON files locally, graph is OFFICE-only
Courier sync protocol unchanged — still JSON over the wire, OFFICE explodes into graph on ingest
Engine choice open: Neo4j (mature, GPL concern), ArangoDB (Apache 2.0, multi-model), DGraph (distributed)
Architecture doc 17 written with full exploration
